stats.headline: Six grammar rules that show up in **almost every PSLE English Booklet A** Grammar MCQ. Master these and you have a strong shot at all 10 marks.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome to Grammar MCQ Mastery
keyConcepts.body: We'll cover the **six grammar rule families** that PSLE tests every single year. If you internalise these, the Grammar MCQ section becomes mostly mechanical.
bullets: **1. Tag questions** — "…, isn't he?" / "…, shall we?"
**2. Noun number rules** — subject-verb agreement + *few/little/many/much*
**3. Pronouns** — reflexive, possessive standalone, relative
**4. Verb forms** — gerund vs infinitive, causative bare infinitive
**5. Connectors & tenses** — *since / although / in spite of* + perfect tenses
**6. Idiomatic prepositions** — *congratulate on*, *robbed of*, *under the impression*
keyConcepts.callout: The pattern in PSLE Booklet A: **one question per rule**, in roughly the order above. Recognise the rule type from the options, then apply.

keyConcepts.title: 1: Tag questions — the mirror rule
keyConcepts.body: A tag question is the little check at the end of a statement: *isn't he?*, *would she?*, *shall we?* The tag must **mirror** the verb/auxiliary of the main statement and **flip the polarity**.
bullets: **Step 1 — Find the main verb/auxiliary.** "She **has** been busy" → tag uses *has*. "He **gave up**" (past simple) → tag uses *did*.
**Step 2 — Flip the polarity.** Positive statement → **negative** tag ("She is busy, **isn't** she?"). Negative statement → **positive** tag ("He hasn't called, **has** he?").
**Step 3 — Match the pronoun.** "Tom" → *he*. "Mary" → *she*. "The team" → *it* (or *they* depending on context).

**Three special cases that catch most students:**
   • **\"Let's\"** → always **\"shall we?\"** (not \"will we?\")
   • **\"I am\"** → **\"aren't I?\"** (irregular)
   • **Negative-meaning words** (never, hardly, seldom, scarcely, nothing) make the statement NEGATIVE → use a **positive** tag

keyConcepts.title: Worked example 1 — Tag questions (PSLE 2024 Q8)
keyConcepts.body: **\"She **never ever** visits the museum, ___?\" asked Mrs Lee.**
bullets: **(1) hasn't she   (2) won't she   (3) does she   (4) will she**

**Step 1 — Identify the verb.** \"visits\" = present simple. Auxiliary for present simple = **does**. So the tag uses *do/does/doesn't*. That rules out *hasn't* (has/have) and *won't / will* (future).
**Step 2 — Check polarity.** \"never ever\" makes the statement **negative**. A negative statement needs a **positive** tag. *doesn't she* would be negative → wrong; *does she* is positive → correct.
**Step 3 — Answer.** **→ Option (3): does she ✓**
keyConcepts.callout: **Mirror the verb, flip the polarity.** When you see *never / hardly / seldom*, the tag flips to positive — even though the statement \"sounds\" positive without a *not*.

keyConcepts.title: 2: Noun number — countable vs uncountable
keyConcepts.body: English splits nouns into **countable** (you can count them: *3 books, 5 ideas*) and **uncountable** (you can't: *water, advice, luck*). Each takes a different family of quantifiers.
bullets: **Countable + plural:** *few, many, several, each, a number of, fewer*
**Uncountable:** *little, much, a great deal of, a large amount of, less*
**Either:** *some, any, enough, more, most, all*

**The treacherous \"mass\" nouns** — these LOOK countable but are uncountable:
   • **time, effort, trouble, advice, luck, news, information**
   • **equipment, furniture, luggage, money, knowledge, work**
For these, always use *little / much / a great deal of*.
**"a great DEAL of" (uncountable)** vs **"a large NUMBER of" (countable)** — the noun *deal* attaches to uncountables, *number* to countables.

keyConcepts.title: 2: Noun number — subject-verb agreement traps
keyConcepts.body: The verb must match the subject's number. PSLE tests **four shapes** where the real subject isn't obvious.
bullets: **Plural-looking but singular**: *news, luggage, mathematics, athletics* → use *is / has*. Example: "The news **has** been circulating since yesterday."
**"The number of X" (singular)** vs **"A number of X" (plural)**. "**The** number of pupils **is** rising." vs "**A** number of pupils **are** absent."
**Interrupters with "as well as / except / together with":** The verb still matches the main subject. "Everyone, except Sanjay and Aidan, **has** handed in their homework."
**Gerund subjects** (verb +ing acting as noun) are always singular: "Reading different types of books **helps** me improve."
**"Neither X nor Y"** matches the noun CLOSEST to the verb. "Neither Jack **nor I** **am** going."

keyConcepts.title: Worked example 2 — Noun number (PSLE 2023 Q5)
keyConcepts.body: **He must have ___ luck as he has won the top prize in the annual lucky draw for the past two years.**
bullets: **(1) many   (2) enough   (3) a great deal of   (4) a large number of**
**Step 1 — Is "luck" countable or uncountable?** UNCOUNTABLE. You can't say *one luck, two lucks*. So we need an uncountable quantifier.

**Step 2 — Eliminate the countable options:**
   • *many* → countable, ✗
   • *a large **number** of* → \"number of\" goes with countables, ✗

**Step 3 — Between *enough* and *a great deal of*:** The sentence implies he won the lucky draw twice — that's clearly **a lot** of luck. *enough* means \"just sufficient\"; *a great deal of* means \"a lot\". \"A great deal of\" fits the meaning.
**Step 4 — Answer.** **→ Option (3): a great deal of ✓**
keyConcepts.callout: **Test the noun first.** If it's *luck, news, advice, time, trouble, effort* — it's uncountable, so reject *many / few / a number of*. Pick from *little / much / a great deal of*.

keyConcepts.title: 3: Pronouns — three families to keep separate
keyConcepts.body: Pronouns get tested in three forms; the trap is usually choosing the right form for the role in the sentence.
bullets: **Personal vs reflexive.** Use the reflexive form (**himself, herself, themselves**) when the action loops back to the SUBJECT. "Mary ate the cake by **herself**." Don't use reflexive when the subject and object are different people.

**Possessive — \"his\" / \"her\" vs \"hers\".** Two forms:
   • **Adjective form** (in front of a noun): *my, your, his, **her**, our, their* → \"**her** car\"
   • **Standalone form** (replaces the noun): *mine, yours, his, **hers**, ours, theirs* → \"That car is **hers**.\"
   ⚠️ No apostrophe ever. \"It's\" = \"it is\". The possessive is \"its\".

**Relative pronouns:**
   • **who** — subject (\"the boy **who** is running\")
   • **whom** — object (\"the boy to **whom** I gave the book\")
   • **whose** — possessive (\"the boy **whose** bag is missing\")
   • **which** — things, not people
**Indefinite pronouns** *everyone, anybody, nobody, someone* are always SINGULAR (they take *is / has*).

keyConcepts.title: Worked example 3 — Pronouns (PSLE 2023 Q4)
keyConcepts.body: **Mrs Phua is someone ___ opinions I truly value.**
bullets: **(1) who   (2) which   (3) whom   (4) whose**
**Step 1 — What role does the pronoun play?** It comes right before "opinions" — the opinions BELONG to Mrs Phua. So it's a **possessive** relative pronoun.

**Step 2 — Eliminate:**
   • *who* — subject (\"who runs\"), ✗
   • *whom* — object (\"to whom I gave\"), ✗
   • *which* — for things, not people, ✗
   • *whose* — possessive ✓
**Step 3 — Answer.** **→ Option (4): whose ✓**
keyConcepts.callout: **Substitute mentally.** \"Mrs Phua is someone **whose** opinions I value\" = \"the opinions OF Mrs Phua, which I value\". When you can replace the relative with *of him/her/them*, it's *whose*.

keyConcepts.title: 4: Verb forms — gerund vs infinitive vs bare
keyConcepts.body: The form of the SECOND verb in a sentence depends on what comes before it. Three main patterns to know.
bullets:
**Verbs followed by GERUND (-ing):**
   enjoy, avoid, mind, consider, suggest, finish, dislike, **have trouble**, **have difficulty**, object to, look forward to, be used to
   → \"Bella enjoys **interacting** with her neighbours.\"

**Verbs followed by INFINITIVE (to + base):**
   want, hope, decide, agree, plan, learn, refuse, manage, expect, seem
   → \"Mohan seems **to have lost** weight.\"

**The PREPOSITION trap.** When \"to\" is a preposition (NOT the infinitive *to*), the next verb is a **gerund**:
   • *object **to** going*, *look forward **to** seeing*
   • *be used **to** working*, *be accustomed **to** doing*
   ⚠️ Students see \"to\" and pick the base form by reflex — that's wrong here.

**Causative / sensory verbs + BARE infinitive (no \"to\"):**
   let, make, help, watch, see, hear, witness, feel, notice
   → \"Kamal **made** his baby sister **giggle**.\"
   → \"Judy **witnessed** a boy **slip** the cards into his bag.\"

keyConcepts.title: Worked example 4 — Verb forms (PSLE 2021 Q7)
keyConcepts.body: **Tom **objected to** ___ on a camping trip as he did not like outdoor activities.**
bullets: **(1) go   (2) going   (3) be going   (4) having gone**
**Step 1 — The trap.** \"object **to**\" looks like the infinitive *to go*. But \"to\" here is a **preposition** (the noun-style \"to something\"), NOT the infinitive marker.
**Step 2 — Test for preposition.** Replace what follows with a NOUN: \"Tom objected to **the trip**.\" Works → \"to\" is a preposition.
**Step 3 — Preposition + verb requires GERUND (-ing).** Pick *going*.
**Step 4 — Answer.** **→ Option (2): going ✓**
keyConcepts.callout: **Memorise the \"to + gerund\" verbs:** *object to, look forward to, be used to, be accustomed to, confess to, admit to*. These five verbs account for almost every \"to + gerund\" PSLE question.

keyConcepts.title: 5: Connectors — cause / concession / time
keyConcepts.body: Each connector signals a specific logical relationship. Read the SECOND clause to figure out which one fits.
bullets: **Cause:** *since, because, as, owing to, due to, because of, in response to*. → "**Since** the concert was cancelled, the organiser refunded us."
**Concession (contradiction)**: *although, even though, despite, in spite of*. The two clauses contradict. → "**In spite of** the continual support, the family is unable to make ends meet."
**Contrast:** *other than, on the other hand, whereas, but*. → "Li Shan does not eat seafood. **Other than** that, she will eat anything."
**Time:** *as soon as, when, while, before, after, until, by the time*. These often dictate the TENSE of the next clause.
**"In spite of / Despite"** must be followed by a NOUN or GERUND, not a clause. "In spite of **being late**" ✓, "In spite of **she was late**" ✗ (use "although" instead).

keyConcepts.title: 5: Conditional inversion & perfect tense triggers
keyConcepts.body: Some grammar questions test **tense triggers** — words that force a specific tense in the next clause.
bullets: **"Had + subject + past participle"** = formal inversion of "if X had…". → "**Had James put** the compass in his bag, we would not be lost" = "If James **had put** the compass in his bag…"
**"wish / if only" + past perfect** for regret. → "Muthu **wished** he **had heeded** his mother's advice."
**"since / for the past N"** + present perfect (continuous). → "The news **has been circulating** since yesterday."
**"by the end of [future time]"** + future perfect. → "By next year, my brother **will have become** a trained instructor."
**"No sooner had X done than Y…"** — past perfect + past simple, with inversion.

keyConcepts.title: Worked example 5 — Inversion (PSLE 2024 Q6)
keyConcepts.body: **'___ James put the compass in his bag, we would not be lost in the forest,' grumbled Ai Ling.**
bullets: **(1) Did   (2) Had   (3) Might   (4) Should**

**Step 1 — Spot the consequence.** \"we **would not be lost**\" = past conditional consequence (a hypothetical missed alternative). This means the protasis (\"if…\") must be in the **past perfect** (\"if James **had put**\").
**Step 2 — Inversion rule.** Formal English drops \"if\" and inverts the auxiliary: \"If James had put\" → \"**Had** James put\". Only **Had** triggers this inversion with past perfect.
**Step 3 — Answer.** **→ Option (2): Had ✓**
keyConcepts.callout: **Trigger phrases for past-perfect inversion:** *Had X done…, Had it not been for…, No sooner had X done…*. When the consequence is \"would (not) have done\" or \"would (not) be\", look for **Had**.

keyConcepts.title: 6: Idiomatic prepositions — fixed combinations
keyConcepts.body: There's no rule — these are FIXED idioms you have to memorise. PSLE picks pairs where multiple options sound vaguely OK.
bullets:
**Verb + preposition pairs** (commit these as a single chunk):
   • congratulate someone **on** something
   • accuse someone **of** something
   • blame someone **for** something
   • rob someone **of** something
   • suspect someone **of** something
   • depend / rely **on** someone
   • succeed **in** doing

**Idiomatic phrases with a fixed preposition:**
   • **under** the impression
   • **in** someone's face (\"shut the door in her face\")
   • **on** purpose, **on** time, **on** behalf of
   • **at** risk, **at** a loss, **at** stake
   • **by** accident, **by** chance, **by** mistake
   • **against** the current, **against** the rules
   • **out of** breath, **out of** stock
**Movement vs position:** *into* (movement) vs *in* (position). "He walked **into** the room" vs "He is **in** the room". Same for *onto / on*, *off of / off*.
**"X minutes into the game"** — "into" with a duration marks a point in time.

keyConcepts.title: Worked example 6 — Idiomatic preposition (PSLE 2024 Q10)
keyConcepts.body: **The police officers were looking for the man who robbed the tourists ___ their possessions.**
bullets: **(1) of   (2) for   (3) off   (4) with**
**Step 1 — Spot the fixed phrase.** The verb is **rob**. The idiom is **"rob someone OF something"** — a fixed pairing.

**Step 2 — Check the others:**
   • *for* — used with *blame* (\"blame him for\"), not *rob*
   • *off* — informal robbery slang, not PSLE-correct
   • *with* — used with weapons (\"robbed with a knife\"), not the stolen item
**Step 3 — Answer.** **→ Option (1): of ✓**
keyConcepts.callout: **Memorise as chunks.** *rob someone OF, accuse someone OF, congratulate someone ON, blame someone FOR.* Don't try to reason them out — they're fixed.

keyConcepts.title: Let's get some practice!
keyConcepts.body: Time to put the six rules together. This quiz pulls real PSLE-style Grammar MCQ questions across all 6 sub-topics.
bullets: **10 questions** — at least one from each sub-topic.
After you submit, the AI flags which rule families you got wrong and suggests a focused follow-up quiz.
**Mistakes are saved** — we'll return to them in an auto-revision session in **1 week**.

cta.label: Click here to start your practice quiz

keyWords.definition: A short check at the end of a statement (isn't he? / shall we?) that mirrors the main verb and flips the polarity.
A noun you can count: book/books, idea/ideas. Takes few/many/several.
A noun that has no plural: water, advice, luck. Takes little/much/a great deal of.
A pronoun that loops back to the subject: himself, herself, themselves. Use when subject = object.
who / whom / whose / which — links a noun to a description (the boy who runs).
The -ing form of a verb used as a noun (running, swimming). Comes after prepositions and certain verbs (enjoy, avoid).
The 'to + base' form (to go, to see). Used after verbs like want, hope, decide, plan.
The base form WITHOUT 'to' (go, see). Used after causative verbs (let, make, help, watch).
Formal alternative to 'if X had…' — flip auxiliary and subject: 'Had X done…'
Connects a dependent clause to a main clause: since, although, when, unless, while.

subTopics.label: Tag questions
subTopics.description: Tag question structure ("isn't he?", "shall we?") — mirroring the main verb and flipping the polarity. Includes negative-word traps (never, hardly, seldom) and irregular cases ("Let's…" → shall we, "I am" → aren't I).

subTopics.label: Noun number rules
subTopics.description: Subject-verb agreement plus countable / uncountable quantifiers. Handles mass nouns (news, luggage, advice), "the number of" vs "a number of", interrupters (everyone except…), gerund subjects.

subTopics.label: Pronouns
subTopics.description: Reflexive (himself, herself), possessive standalone (his, hers), and relative pronouns (who/whom/whose/which). Tests when each form is required by the role of the pronoun in the sentence.

subTopics.label: Verb forms
subTopics.description: Gerund vs infinitive vs bare infinitive after a verb. Includes the "to + gerund" preposition trap (object to going, look forward to seeing) and causative bare infinitive (made her giggle, witnessed him slip).

subTopics.label: Connectors & tense markers
subTopics.description: Cause / concession / contrast / time connectors (since, although, in spite of, when) plus tense triggers — conditional inversion ("Had X done…"), wish + past perfect, since + present perfect continuous.

subTopics.label: Idiomatic prepositions
subTopics.description: Fixed verb + preposition combinations (congratulate ON, rob OF, accuse OF, blame FOR) and idiomatic phrases (under the impression, in someone's face, against the current, by accident).
